summary: Gruber amplifies Fraser Speirs's explanation of why manually clearing the iOS multitasking bar is unnecessary and misguided. The multitasking bar is not a list of running apps but simply a history of recently used apps, similar to browser history. iOS automatically suspends and removes apps from memory as needed, making manual clearing pointless busywork. Gruber is particularly frustrated that Apple's own Genius Bar employees are spreading this misinformation to customers. He acknowledges rare edge cases where killing a specific stuck app is warranted, but insists routine clearing provides zero benefit.
key_points: The iOS multitasking bar is a recent-app history list, not a task manager showing running processes — apps persist in it even after a full device restart
iOS automatically handles suspending and removing apps from memory, which is the entire design philosophy behind the lack of a Quit command
Apple's own Genius Bar employees are spreading the false advice to manually clear the multitasking bar, undermining the system's design intent
Manually clearing the app list is described as 'voodoo placebo' — users who swear by it are experiencing confirmation bias, not real performance gains
money_quotes: Emptying this list of applications is simply needless, mindless, busywork.
Manually zapping all apps from this list is a voodoo placebo.
The whole point of iOS's multitasking model is that you, the user, should not have to worry about managing which applications are running and which are not.
If you were supposed to do that, apps would have a Quit command. They don't.
It is simply a list of your most recently used applications, whether they're running in the background, suspended in memory, or completely inactive.
Like with any voodoo, there are die-hard believers.
Shame on the 'geniuses' who are peddling this advice.
key_insight: iOS was specifically designed so users never need to manage running apps, and the multitasking bar is just a usage history — not a task manager — making routine clearing a superstitious waste of time.
